title: Rethinking Aquifer Characterization: Insights from Lagging Models

research.question: Are the two lagging parameters needed to explain pumping-test drawdown, and can their fitted values reveal whether inertia or microstructural interactions control different parts of an aquifer?
research.system: Confined and unconfined aquifers represented by pumping wells and observation wells in fractured, weathered, and heterogeneous settings in India, the United States, and Taiwan.
research.inputData: Time-drawdown observations from the Maheshwaram Watershed, Spearfish, Dongju Island, eastern Massachusetts, and Rapid City, including multiwell spatial data for the last two sites.
research.method: Fit a lagging groundwater-flow model with storage coefficient, transmissivity, flux lag, and gradient lag; compare it with the non-lagging model; interpolate fitted parameters spatially; and derive lagging-ratio and characteristic-time diagnostics.
research.benchmark: Traditional Darcy-based pumping models, with model adequacy judged by time-drawdown fit, RMSE, AIC, and BIC and characteristic time compared with the Darcy value.
research.uncertainty: Copula conditional distributions provide 5th-to-95th percentile widths for 90% confidence intervals on spatially interpolated parameters; fitted parameters remain radius-of-influence averages rather than point properties.
research.conclusion: Across the examined cases, the lagging model better represented drawdown despite two additional parameters; the lagging ratio separated inertia-dominated from microstructure-dominated regions, while characteristic-time comparisons showed that Darcy models can misstate equilibration time.

narrative.throughline: A delay between hydraulic gradient and groundwater flux becomes a spatial diagnostic rather than an extra pair of opaque fitting parameters.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience knows that pumping-test drawdown is commonly interpreted with Darcy-based transmissivity and storage.
narrative.stakes: If delayed responses are forced into an instantaneous Darcy model, fitted aquifer properties and the time to near-steady conditions can be misinterpreted.
narrative.resolution: Fit and test the lagging model, convert its two delays into a lagging-ratio map and characteristic time, and return those diagnostics to the monitored well fields with explicit uncertainty and field-investigation limits.

background.premise: Lagging theory conceptually permits groundwater flux and hydraulic gradient to occur at different times in complex media.
background.visibleEvidence: A conceptual timing diagram places the hydraulic-gradient and groundwater-flux peaks at different times.
background.whyNow: This temporal mismatch is the physical puzzle that an instantaneous Darcy relation cannot display.
background.boundary: The paper notes that the classical approximation is usually adequate and focuses on settings where delayed behavior is consequential.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.1, Equations (1)-(2) and Figure 1
background.sourceExcerpt: between groundwater flow and hydraulic gradient does not occur
background.visualObject: Conceptual paired flux and hydraulic-gradient pulses above a pumping-well cross-section.
background.motionPurpose: Offset the conceptual pulse peaks to make the constitutive delay visible before introducing lag parameters.
background.stepDetail: Start with coincident Darcy pulses; hold the gradient pulse fixed; shift the flux pulse to illustrate the lagging-theory relation.
background.transitionBridge: The separated pulse peaks become the two lag times used by the model.

background.premise: Adding two lagging parameters creates both an over-parameterization question and an interpretation question.
background.visibleEvidence: A four-parameter lagging fit and a two-parameter Darcy fit are placed beside a map with initially unlabeled delay fields.
background.whyNow: The model is useful only if the extra parameters improve fit enough to justify complexity and can be translated into hydrological meaning.
background.boundary: Better fit alone does not prove that fitted delays are unique local material properties.
background.sourceExcerpt: Despite the promising potential of the lagging model to enhance
background.visualObject: Competing parameter sets connected to one observed drawdown curve and one spatial well map.
background.motionPurpose: Split one concern into model-selection evidence and physical-interpretation evidence without conflating them.
background.stepDetail: Reveal the two extra delay parameters; attach AIC/BIC to the fit branch; attach lagging ratio and characteristic time to the interpretation branch.
background.transitionBridge: The two unanswered branches define the subsequent fitting and diagnostic operations.

methods.input: Darcy flux and hydraulic-gradient traces over pumping time
methods.output: A constitutive relation with flux lag tau_q and gradient lag tau_s
methods.validity: The lagging formulation retains a linear flux-gradient relation while permitting a temporal offset, following the cited dual-phase-lag analogy.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The lagging theory (Lin and Yeh, 2017) draws inspiration from
methods.visualObject: Two pulse peaks bracketed by tau_q and tau_s, then compressed into the lagged constitutive relation.
methods.motionPurpose: Turn the visible timing offsets into named model parameters.
methods.stepDetail: Mark the flux peak; mark the gradient peak; attach tau_q and tau_s to their respective offsets; replace the pulse annotations with the lagged law.
methods.transitionBridge: The two conceptual time offsets become the lag parameters entering the pumping model.

methods.input: The lag-time pair tau_q and tau_s
methods.output: Lagging ratio lambda with a threshold at one
methods.validity: The paper defines lambda as tau_q divided by tau_s and uses one as the boundary between the two lag regimes.
methods.sourceExcerpt: parameters. The first is the lagging ratio
methods.visualObject: The two lag brackets collapse into a ratio gauge centered on lambda equals one.
methods.motionPurpose: Convert the relative delay into a single dimensionless regime indicator.
methods.stepDetail: Place tau_q above tau_s as a ratio; form lambda; mark lambda equals one; preserve the two sides of the gauge.
methods.transitionBridge: A separate time-scale diagnostic is formed from the lag pair and aquifer geometry in M03.

methods.input: Lag times tau_q and tau_s with storage transmissivity and radial well distance
methods.validity: The paper defines tau_c as the time scale for equilibrium or substantial water-level change and shows its Darcy limit when the lags are equal.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The second parameter we introduce is the characteristic time
methods.visualObject: A radial pumping-well influence zone closes into a clock labeled tau_c.
methods.motionPurpose: Turn aquifer diffusivity distance and lag terms into one equilibration-time object.
methods.stepDetail: Show the radial distance; attach storage and transmissivity; insert the lag terms; resolve the combined expression into the tau_c clock.
methods.transitionBridge: The lag parameters and aquifer properties are estimated from observed drawdown in M04.

methods.input: Observed time-drawdown series at each pumping or observation well
methods.output: Best-fit S, T, tau_q, and tau_s for each analyzed well
methods.validity: Least squares with the trust region reflective algorithm minimizes observed-minus-modeled drawdown residuals under the lagging model.
methods.sourceExcerpt: In our approach, we utilize the least squares method in conjunction
methods.visualObject: Observed drawdown points and a model curve whose four parameter handles move until residual stems shorten.
methods.motionPurpose: Show parameter estimation as residual reduction rather than a black-box fit.
methods.stepDetail: Display observed points; draw residual stems to an initial curve; adjust the four handles together through TRF iterations; settle on the minimum-residual curve.
methods.transitionBridge: The lagging-model fit is paired with a separately fitted classical Darcy baseline in M05.

methods.input: Observed time-drawdown series and the model without lagging parameters
methods.output: Baseline storage transmissivity and no-lag drawdown responses
methods.validity: The paper applies both the lagging model and the model without lagging effects to the same time-drawdown data sets before comparing model adequacy.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.4 Massachusetts field analysis, paragraph preceding the RMSE comparison
methods.sourceExcerpt: We applied both the lagging model and the model without lagging
methods.visualObject: Observed drawdown points receiving a separate two-parameter classical Darcy response curve.
methods.motionPurpose: Fit the no-lag baseline on the same observations before any information criterion compares model complexity.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the observed points; initialize storage and transmissivity; reduce no-lag residuals; preserve the fitted baseline and its likelihood.
methods.transitionBridge: Both independently fitted models now supply residual evidence for explicit likelihood mapping in M06.

methods.input: Independently fitted lagging and Darcy responses with their residual evidence and parameter counts
methods.output: Lagging-model and Darcy-model likelihood terms paired with their parameter counts
methods.validity: The source defines likelihood and parameter count as separate inputs to both AIC and BIC so they must remain explicit before the criteria are evaluated.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 3.1 Field implementation in India, AIC and BIC definitions
methods.visualObject: Two fitted response panels each releasing a likelihood token beside its parameter-count token
methods.motionPurpose: Keep goodness-of-fit evidence and model size explicit before either information criterion combines them.
methods.stepDetail: Retain both fitted responses; attach each model likelihood term; attach each parameter count; preserve the paired inputs for both criteria.
methods.transitionBridge: The likelihood and parameter-count pairs enter the AIC and BIC comparison in M07.

methods.input: Lagging-model and Darcy-model likelihoods with their parameter counts
methods.output: AIC and BIC evidence for or against the two added lag parameters
methods.validity: Both criteria penalize model complexity, with BIC imposing the stronger additional-parameter penalty.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Lower AIC and BIC values indicate a better balance between
methods.visualObject: Two fit curves feed into paired AIC and BIC scales carrying explicit complexity weights.
methods.motionPurpose: Make the improvement-versus-complexity test visible for the lagging and Darcy alternatives.
methods.stepDetail: Send each model likelihood to both criteria; add its parameter-count penalty; compare the resulting paired values with lower values highlighted.
methods.transitionBridge: The selected lagging fits provide the wellwise parameters interpolated in M08.

methods.input: Lagging-parameter estimates at irregularly spaced observation wells
methods.output: Spatial fields of S, T, tau_q, and tau_s
methods.validity: Copula interpolation was selected to represent non-Gaussian dependence and spatial anomalies that conventional kriging can miss.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3; PDF pages 5-8, Figures 7-8 and 11-12
methods.sourceExcerpt: In this study, we adopted copula interpolation due to its ability to model non-Gaussian dependencies
methods.visualObject: Discrete well markers expand into four continuous parameter maps while retaining the well locations.
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal how point estimates become spatially coherent aquifer-property fields.
methods.stepDetail: Place fitted values at wells; form copula-conditioned distributions on a grid; color each grid cell by its conditional estimate; preserve wells as visible anchors.
methods.transitionBridge: The copula interpolation state is retained to construct a conditional CDF at every grid point in M09.

methods.input: Copula interpolation state for each parameter at every spatial grid point
methods.output: One conditional parameter cumulative distribution at each interpolation grid point
methods.validity: The source obtains conditional parameter distributions through copula interpolation before extracting percentile bounds for confidence intervals.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.4 Massachusetts field analysis, paragraph preceding Figure 8
methods.sourceExcerpt: conditional distributions obtained through copula interpola- tion
methods.visualObject: Each interpolation grid cell opening into a parameter-specific conditional cumulative distribution
methods.motionPurpose: Produce the probability object from which spatial confidence bounds are later read.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the copula state; condition on each grid location; accumulate parameter probability; preserve one CDF per parameter and cell.
methods.transitionBridge: The conditional CDFs expose their 5th and 95th percentiles for width calculation in M10.

methods.input: Conditional parameter distributions at each interpolation grid point
methods.validity: The interval bounds are the 5th and 95th percentiles of each conditional cumulative distribution.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.4 and Figure 8; PDF page 7, Figure 12
methods.sourceExcerpt: Specifically, for each grid point, we compute the 5th and 95th percentiles from the conditional cumulative distribution function of the parameter values, representing the lower and upper bounds of the 90% confidence interval.
methods.visualObject: Each map cell opens into a conditional CDF with 5th and 95th percentile brackets, then returns as an interval-width map.
methods.motionPurpose: Represent spatial uncertainty as visible width rather than a prose qualifier.
methods.stepDetail: Reveal one grid-cell CDF; mark its 5th percentile; mark its 95th percentile; measure the bracket width; propagate that width encoding across the map.
methods.transitionBridge: Uncertainty remains attached to the same spatial grid used for regime interpretation.

methods.input: Spatial tau_q and tau_s fields with their confidence-width context
methods.output: A lambda map partitioned into inertia-dominated and microstructure-dominated regions
methods.validity: The paper defines lambda=1 as the threshold, with lambda below one indicating inertia and lambda above one indicating microstructural interactions.
methods.locator: PDF pages 5-7, Sections 3.4-3.5 and Figures 9 and 13
methods.sourceExcerpt: This figure facilitates the identification of areas where inertia dominates
methods.visualObject: The two lag-time maps divide cell by cell into one thresholded lambda regime map.
methods.motionPurpose: Make the physical interpretation emerge from the fitted spatial fields.
methods.stepDetail: Pair tau_q and tau_s at each cell; form lambda; draw the lambda=1 contour; label only the two resulting physical regimes.
methods.transitionBridge: The regime map is retained while the classical Darcy characteristic time is calculated in M12.

methods.input: Sitewise storage transmissivity and pumping-well radial distance
methods.output: Classical Darcy diffusion time for every analyzed site
methods.validity: The source gives the Darcy characteristic time as radial distance squared times storage divided by transmissivity when the two lag times are equal.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.2 Quantification and characterization of lagging parameter effects, Equation 4 and following paragraph
methods.visualObject: Each site's radius storage and transmissivity resolving into one classical Darcy time marker
methods.motionPurpose: Produce the classical time scale independently before comparing it with the lag-adjusted characteristic time.
methods.stepDetail: Retain each site radius; square it; apply storage and transmissivity; preserve one Darcy time marker per site.
methods.transitionBridge: The calculated Darcy times align site by site with the lag-adjusted times in M13.

methods.input: Sitewise lag-adjusted and Darcy characteristic times
methods.output: Scatter evidence showing how tau_c departs from the Darcy time scale
methods.validity: The paper plots both characteristic-time estimates for all analyzed sites and evaluates their relation to the one-to-one line.
methods.sourceExcerpt: plotted as scatter plots, as shown in Fig. 14
methods.visualObject: Sitewise tau_c points plotted against Darcy characteristic time with a one-to-one line.
methods.motionPurpose: Make the revised equilibration time visible as departures from the classical reference line.
methods.stepDetail: Place the one-to-one line; add the site estimates; reveal the fitted relation; retain the departure spread.
methods.transitionBridge: The time comparison and lag-regime maps return to the monitored aquifers and their interpretation.

symbols.visualObject: Time bracket attached to the groundwater-flux pulse
symbols.meaning: Flux lag associated with delayed specific discharge
symbols.transition: Grow the bracket from the flux peak to its reference time

symbols.visualObject: Time bracket attached to the hydraulic-gradient pulse
symbols.meaning: Hydraulic-gradient or storage lag associated with pore-structure response
symbols.transition: Grow the bracket from the gradient peak to its reference time

symbols.visualObject: Threshold gauge built from the two lag brackets
symbols.meaning: Lagging ratio used to distinguish inertia-dominated and microstructure-dominated flow regimes
symbols.transition: Stack the two lag brackets into a ratio and mark one as the threshold

symbols.visualObject: Clock attached to the radial pumping-well influence zone
symbols.meaning: Characteristic time for groundwater equilibration or substantial head change under the lagging model
symbols.transition: Transform the radial diffusion path into a clock face

symbols.visualObject: Storage fill level within the pumping-well influence zone
symbols.transition: Label the stored-water fill after the aquifer object is visible

symbols.visualObject: Radial transmissive pathway from the pumping well
symbols.transition: Color-match the pathway to the T term in the characteristic-time expression

return.visualObject: The Massachusetts and Rapid City well maps carrying lambda regimes, 90% interval-width texture, and a tau_c versus Darcy-time inset.
return.claim: For the examined pumping tests, the extra lag parameters were supported by lower information criteria and yielded interpretable spatial regimes and revised characteristic times.
return.claimBoundary: The fitted properties are spatial averages over each well's radius of influence, and the lagging model remains a simplified complementary tool rather than a replacement for detailed field characterization.
return.locator: PDF page 9, Sections 3.6-3.7 and 4, concluding remarks
return.sourceExcerpt: The lagging model, while beneficial, should therefore be seen as a complementary tool, not a replacement for detailed field investigations.
return.minimalText: A diagnostic layer, not a field substitute

visual.primaryObject: Pumping-well drawdown curves linked to mapped observation wells
visual.accentObject: Lagging-ratio threshold contour and characteristic-time clock
visual.measuredQuantity: Drawdown through time with fitted lag times and spatial confidence widths
visual.geometryNotes: Begin with paired temporal pulses, preserve the pumping-well radius as the characteristic-time geometry, transform discrete well estimates into copula grids, and end on the original site maps rather than on isolated equations.
